text.title: Clean Architecture Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding Clean Architecture. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale. The {skip-this-methodology} rule routes inapplicable cases.

rule.statement: Source-code dependencies MUST point inward across layers (frameworks → interface adapters → use cases → entities). Inner layers MUST NOT import outer layers; dependency-inversion is enforced by interfaces declared in inner layers and implemented in outer.

rationale.source: Robert C. Martin, Clean Architecture (2017); Hexagonal Architecture (Cockburn, 2005)

rule.rationale: Outward dependencies couple business logic to frameworks and IO. Inverting the direction lets you swap web framework, ORM, or transport without rewriting use cases.

rule.statement: Every domain entity MUST have a test suite that runs in under 100ms total with zero database, HTTP, or framework dependencies. Entity tests use only in-memory state.

rationale.source: Robert C. Martin, Clean Architecture (2017); domain-test surveys

rule.rationale: If entity tests need a DB, the entity has framework coupling — outer-layer concerns have leaked into the inner ring. Sub-100ms suites also keep TDD viable.

rule.statement: Domain events MUST be published only after the entity is persisted. Either commit first then publish, or use a transactional outbox pattern to guarantee no-loss-on-failure.
rule.rationale: Publishing before commit produces ghost events for failed writes; commit before publish loses events on crash. Outbox closes the gap.

rule.statement: Use-case transactions MUST modify exactly one aggregate root. If a flow needs two aggregates, it commits them via two separate repository.save() calls and handles partial-failure explicitly.

rationale.source: Vaughn Vernon, Implementing DDD (2013); aggregate consistency rules

rule.rationale: Multi-aggregate transactions create distributed-lock contention and violate Evans' aggregate boundary rule. Two-call patterns force the engineer to model the failure path.

rule.statement: Mappers convert domain entities to DTOs at the layer boundary. If a mapper exceeds 10 lines, the layer boundary is wrong — either consolidate the types or split the layer.

rationale.source: Clean Architecture mapping patterns; pydantic/dataclass mapping conventions

rule.rationale: Mappers grow into business logic if unconstrained. The 10-line limit is a forcing function to reconsider the boundary.

rule.statement: Skip when the code is a CRUD prototype with no domain logic, or when the team has explicitly chosen Transaction Script over Domain Model.

rationale.source: Martin Fowler, Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (2002)

rule.rationale: Clean Architecture adds layers and indirection that pay off only when domain logic exists to protect. CRUD-only apps pay the cost without benefit.
